breakdown_by_role.rationale: Plan building, audience research, RFP generation, flighting, pacing, optimization and reporting are heavily AI-exposed — native platform automation (Google DV360, The Trade Desk AI) already does most of this; cross-platform complexity and vendor negotiation persist. Proxy OEWS 41-3011 (8.18%) + 13-1161 (6.55%) = 14.73% of wage bill, but actual media-buying firms are dominated by planner/buyer/investment staff — reconstructed to 35% per prompt guidance. Within-role fraction 0.50 (midpoint of prompt's 0.40-0.60 range).

Trafficking, tag management, make-goods, discrepancy reconciliation and media billing are the most directly automatable cost lines. AI and workflow tools (campaign manager APIs, automated reconciliation) already handle the bulk of this. Not in OEWS top-10; estimated at 10-15% per prompt guidance; using 12% midpoint.

Client relationships, media-vendor rate leverage (scale-based, survives acquisition) and principal deal-making persist; internal management and reporting automate. Proxy OEWS 11-1021 (9.26%) + 11-2021 (5.20%) = 14.46%; reconstructed to 16% for actual firms. Many firms are founder-led given n_total=663.

Attribution, MMM-lite reporting and dashboarding automate heavily via AI tools (Rockerbox, Northbeam, etc.); measurement design judgment and client-specific analytics interpretation persist. Not in OEWS top-10; estimated 6-10% per prompt; using 8% midpoint.

Status reporting, plan presentations, and performance decks automate; relationship ownership and strategic counsel persist. Proxy OEWS 41-3091 (3.02%) underestimates actual client-facing roles in buying shops; reconstructed to 9%.

breakdown_by_role.role: Back office and remaining tail (incl. proxy contaminants)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Finance/billing admin in a business that reconciles high-volume media invoices — strongly automatable. The 4-digit 5418 proxy contaminates with PR specialists (8.29%), Graphic Designers (3.30%), Demonstrators (1.62%), Merchandise Displayers (1.21%) — none present in media buying shops. These contaminating roles plus true finance/admin sum to ~20% of the reconstructed wage bill. AI-driven accounts-payable/receivable, media invoice reconciliation tools address most of this.

pi_dist.rationale: Dominant pricing model is commission on media spend (typically 3-15% of billings) and fixed-fee retainers benchmarked to spend. This is pricing-model-favorable: AI cost savings flow to the operator as margin because the billing basis is spend (not hours). However, the client-facing deflation channel is commission-rate pressure via fee auditors (Ebiquity, Accenture Media) and client procurement departments — ANA documented that 1/3 of advertisers revised media agency contracts in a recent year. Additionally, platform self-serve (Google PMax, Meta Advantage+) directly threatens the commission base itself for SMB/mid-market clients, the core market for these 663 firms. Client relationships are partially firm-level (buying clout and vendor relationships) and partially individual-level (account director relationships). Principal-based buying margin — the highest-capture channel — is structurally inaccessible to band-sized independents lacking holdco-scale inventory commitments. Net: moderate firm control, with ongoing erosion channel through rate compression and direct bypass.
sources: ANA Media Agency Compensation Practices 2019 (commission structure data)
ANA Programmatic Media Supply Chain Transparency Study 2023-2024
Marketing Dive: ANA one-third of advertisers revised contracts
AdExchanger: The Rise of Principal Media 2024

pi_moat.rationale: No licensure requirement. Switching costs are moderate: established media vendor rate leverage (reach-scale dependent), platform fee structures, workflow integration (DSPs, ad servers), and client onboarding friction. However, the moat is under active compression from (1) Google PMax and Meta Advantage+ targeting exactly the SMB/mid-market clients of these 663 firms; (2) retail media self-serve consoles reducing the cross-channel complexity that justified agency involvement; (3) absence of proprietary data moats at band-scale. The realistic threat is direct platform bypass, not just commodity competition. Score reflects meaningful but eroding switching costs with no hard barrier.
sources: eMarketer: FAQ on AI media buying 2026
MediaPost: Hey Agencies, Meta and Google Are Taking Your Business 2024
IAB State of Data 2025
JP Morgan: How Advertising Agencies Compete in 2026

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: IAB State of Data 2025 (March 2025) found 30% of agencies/brands/publishers have fully integrated AI across media campaign lifecycle. Historical analog: programmatic advertising went from ~10% adoption (2012) to crossing ~50% adoption in approximately 2015-2016, a span of ~4 years from early adoption; eMarketer data shows US programmatic hit ~45% of digital display spend in 2014 and exceeded 60% by 2018, placing the 50% crossing at 2015-2016 (4-5 years from 2011 baseline). AI adoption in agencies is accelerating faster: IAB's 2025 study found 'half of those who haven't fully integrated AI expect to do it by 2026,' implying ~65% fully integrated by end of 2026. On this trajectory 50% is crossed approximately 2025-2026. Taking conservative view (smaller firms lag, 'fully integrated' overstates 'materially adopting'), t50 = 3.0 years (mid-2029). Plausible range: 1.5-5 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: IAB State of Data 2025
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Only 30% of agencies, brands, and publishers have fully integrated AI across the media campaign lifecycle

historical_analogs.value: 4-5 years
historical_analogs.source: eMarketer / MarTech (programmatic advertising adoption 2012-2016)
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Programmatic ad spend hit $10B in 2014, approximately 45% of digital display; reached 61%+ by 2018 — crossing 50% adoption circa 2015-2016, approximately 4 years from 2011-2012 baseline

owners_60plus_pct.source: Oberlo / SBA Advocacy (general small business owner age data)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 37% of small business owners are between 55-73 years old in 2024; share of self-employed age 65+ rose from 13.0% (2013) to 16.3% (2023) per Census/SBA

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No succession shortage specifically documented for NAICS 541830 media buying agencies. General small business data cited; agency-industry-specific owner age surveys (Ad Age Agency Report) do not publish 60+ breakdowns. Estimate of 35% over 60 based on general small business demographics (37% aged 55-73) adjusted for the fact that many media buying agencies were founded in the 1990s-2000s digital-advertising expansion era by owners now in their 50s-60s.

active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE-backed performance and digital platforms including Tinuiti, Wpromote, Power Digital, Bounteous, and New Engen roll up specialty agencies; marketing services M&A rose 14% YTD 2025. Specific media-buying (541830) PE platforms are fewer — performance media roll-ups (Tinuiti, Power Digital, New Engen) overlap with this code.

buy_mult.source: Axial.net / First Page Sage marketing agency valuation multiples 2025
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Small agencies $1-2M EBITDA: 3.5x-5x; average ~4.5x for agencies around $1-3M EBITDA. TobinLeff data: ~$500K EBITDA averages 3.33x. Using 4.5x as representative of band-sized (10-99 employee class) media buying agency acquisitions.

exit_mult.source: First Page Sage / Axial / JEGI Clarity M&A data 2025
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Agencies $5-10M EBITDA: 8.1x-10.6x; larger agencies (10M+ EBITDA platform exits) typically 8x-10x for generalist media services. Using 8.5x for performance media platforms given AI/disintermediation risk discount vs. higher-growth digital specialties.

pricing_structure.value: Commission on media spend (3-15% of billings); fixed-fee retainers benchmarked to spend; some principal-based trading margin at holdco scale. Commission dominant for independents.
pricing_structure.source: ANA Media Agency Compensation Practices; ANA Programmatic Transparency studies 2023-2024
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: 40% of respondents use commissions for programmatic media; 24% for digital media buying; 12% for general agency services (ANA 2024 programmatic benchmark). Commission and % of spend models dominate independent media agency billing.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 14.18pp of revenue freed. A = 3.33 (< 5). The cross-check tension condition (uplift < 3pp AND A >= 5) does not apply: uplift is 14.18pp which far exceeds 3pp. Consistent — high V with moderate-to-slow adoption speed.

terminal_value.justification: The terminal state is not full disintermediation but meaningful demand compression. Two distinct mechanisms: (1) AI cuts operator costs — commission/% of spend model means AI efficiency on internal labor (planning, trafficking, analytics) flows to operator as margin, not to clients. This is the favorable case. (2) AI replaces the operator's product — Google PMax and Meta Advantage+ automate the buy-side for the SMB/mid-market clients these 663 firms serve, shrinking the spend that flows through independent media buyers. The terminal scenario is a smaller number of firms managing complex cross-channel campaigns and principal-deal inventory for mid-market advertisers that exceed self-serve capability thresholds, while the long tail of SMB spend routes directly through platform AI. This is PRESSURED not MELTING: some demand for independent, cross-platform, vendor-neutral media expertise survives mature AI (especially for clients with $1M+ media budgets across 5+ channels), but the market shrinks significantly from today's base. The n_total=663 universe likely contracts to ~200-300 firms over 10 years.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from the reconstructed role structure (prompt-guided) applied to the labor_share dataset input — no reference to M&A deal activity or PE consolidation trends. A was derived from IAB State of Data 2025 (current adoption survey) and eMarketer programmatic adoption historical analog — no reference to deal activity. Confirmed: V and A are grounded in AI capability and adoption evidence only.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: commission on media spend (~40% of transactions per ANA 2024) and fixed fees benchmarked to spend (~35%); principal-based trading margin (~15%, mostly at holdco scale, not available to band-sized independents). The commission/% of spend model means AI efficiency flows TO THE OPERATOR as margin (the billing basis is spend, not hours — AI reducing planning hours does not mechanically reduce the commission). The deflation channel is instead: (a) fee auditors (Ebiquity, MediaLink) compressing commission rates via contract renegotiation — ANA found 1/3 of advertisers revised contracts recently; (b) platform self-serve reducing the spend that flows through agencies entirely. This is reflected in pi_dist=0.55 (moderate: operator keeps AI savings as margin but faces commission-rate pressure and base erosion) and pi_moat=0.35 (meaningful but eroding switching costs; platform bypass is a real mechanism). The principal-based margin trap for small independents is noted: they cannot access the highest-capture model, so their margin structure is more exposed.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=1.5 years: A=6.67 → S≈4.08; at t50=5.0 years: A=2.0 → S≈3.33. Range of approximately ±0.37 around base S=3.73.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0.45-0.58 (driven by media planner/buyer and back-office fraction uncertainty)
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At 0.45: V_raw=0.1242, V=4.97, S≈3.64; at 0.58: V_raw=0.1600, V=6.40, S≈3.80. Range of approximately ±0.08 around base S=3.73.
At 0.25: C=1.375, S≈3.57; at 0.45: C=2.475, S≈3.91. Range of approximately ±0.17 around base S=3.73.

evidence.reviewer_note: Labor_share caveat: the 0.2759 figure is computed on SUSB gross billings receipts (not net commission income). If net revenue is ~25-35% of gross billings (a typical gross-to-net ratio for commission-based media buying), the true labor_share on a net-revenue basis would be 3-4x higher (0.7-1.0+), implying V_raw would be capped at 1.0 and V=10. However, the dataset note already addresses this: the SUSB receipts figure for 541830 ($9B) and revenue/employee ($435K) suggest the SUSB is capturing mostly commission income (net), not gross billings pass-through — otherwise revenue/employee would be $2M-4M, which is holdco-scale. This creates ambiguity: if SUSB receipts approximate net commission revenue, then V=5.67 is reasonable; if there is substantial billings pass-through contamination, V could be higher. The score is reported at face value per dataset rules; this conflict is flagged here. Role-mix contamination: the 4-digit 5418 proxy (OEWS) was explicitly reconstructed per prompt guidance to remove PR specialists, graphic designers, demonstrators, and merchandise displayers — not present in actual media buying shops. The ai_replaceable_share breakdown uses reconstructed roles, not raw OEWS wage shares. Active consolidators (3) is an estimate reflecting performance-media-adjacent PE platforms; no dedicated 541830-only roll-up platform was identified in research — the true active count may be 1-4.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Quoting, job specs and proof approvals automate substantially; client relationships with agencies, nonprofits and financial-services mailers are often personal to the owner-operator and persist. 40% automation fraction reflecting mix of automatable transactional work and non-automatable relationship work.

5418 contamination — these roles barely exist in direct mail houses. Where present (campaign reporting, audience segmentation summaries), they are near-fully automatable. 65% fraction (slightly lower than maximum to allow for minimal client-presentation judgment).

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists (13-1161)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Data services and list management are the white-collar core of a direct mail house and the most AI-exposed function. List hygiene, merge/purge, NCOA processing, segmentation and response modeling are near-fully automatable by current AI. 75% fraction — the 25% residual covers data strategy and client customization judgment.

Plant scheduling, postal-rate optimization and job costing automate substantially; customer escalation and vendor negotiation persist and cannot be automated. 40% fraction balancing automatable operational analytics vs non-automatable interpersonal management.

Direct mail creative, copy and versioning are directly AI-generable; many houses already template creative. 75% fraction — high because direct mail creative is formulaic (headlines, offers, calls to action in fixed formats), with 25% residual for bespoke brand work.
Primarily in-person promotional roles; physical presence cannot be automated. Minimal 10% for scheduling and administrative components only.
Similar to advertising sales agents: quoting, job specifications, and follow-up automate; client relationships persist. Same 40% fraction as 41-3011.
Physical display work; cannot be automated. Minimal 5% for scheduling components only.

Campaign reporting, analytics and internal briefing automate; marketing strategy, client relationship management, and postal-logistics decision-making persist. 45% reflecting mix of automatable administrative and non-automatable strategic functions.

Direct mail creative direction — standard formats, offer-driven layouts, personalized versioning — is more templated than general advertising and therefore more automatable. 65% fraction (higher than OOH equivalent at 60%) reflecting direct mail's formulaic creative requirements.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unlisted roles (production/lettershop operations, admin/back-office, data staff)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Unlisted 49.3% covers: (1) production/lettershop physical workers — press operators (51-5112), mail machine operators (43-9051), bindery/insertion crews (~35% of total wage bill, 0% automatable: physical print, inserting, sorting and postal logistics are machine-operated but human-run; not AI-automatable); (2) admin/back-office — order entry, billing, postal paperwork, CSR (~15% of total wage bill, ~80% automatable by current AI for order management and statements of mailing). Blended fraction: (0.35×0 + 0.15×0.80) / 0.493 = 0.12/0.493 = 0.2434 ≈ 0.24. The 0.2807 labor share corroborates the production-heavy character (heavy materials/postage pass-through reduces labor share).

pi_dist.rationale: Direct mail jobs are bid per-piece with postage passed through at cost. When AI collapses data-prep, creative and admin cost lines, competitive bidding among mail houses passes savings straight to clients as lower per-piece prices — this is the deflation channel. Agencies route jobs to lowest-bidding shop, further weakening firm-level client ownership. Clients (or their agencies) can use generic AI to do list hygiene, segmentation, creative versioning themselves, collapsing the white-collar value-added margin while leaving only low-margin physical print-and-mail execution. pi_dist set low at 0.30 reflecting: (a) per-piece competitive bidding makes cost savings fully visible to buyers; (b) agency intermediation means the firm does not own the client relationship; (c) AI bypass of white-collar services is technically feasible and increasingly practiced by sophisticated mailers.
sources: USPS FY2025 Annual Report: Marketing Mail 56,756M pieces at $15,734M revenue; 7.38% rate increase July 2025 — postage is the dominant cost, operator cannot control it

ctacquisitions.com printing valuation 2026: 'direct mail/variable-data with strong recurring marketing-services pull-through trades 5.5-6.5x EBITDA' vs 3.5-4.5x for general print — value-added services are the only premium, and AI can replicate them
Pragmatic CRM (2025): USPS postage rate increase analysis noting direct mailers absorbing 7.38% rate increase — operator has no pricing leverage on dominant cost line

pi_moat.rationale: Minimal moat barriers for direct mail houses: (1) No licensure required to operate a mail house; (2) Physical equipment (presses, inserters, mail sorters) is commodity-priced and available at auction; (3) CASS certification (postal address accuracy) and NCOA processing agreements are modest switching costs but widely available; (4) AI makes the white-collar differentiation layer (data hygiene, creative, versioning) replicable by clients or any competitor; (5) secular USPS Marketing Mail volume decline (40% from 2008 to 2025, down to 56.8B pieces) shrinks the revenue base over time. Moat set at 0.25 reflecting only the physical production plant (real equipment investment, space, postal relationship) as a meaningful barrier; all white-collar services are bypassable.
sources: USPS FY2025 Annual Report: Marketing Mail 56,756M pieces FY2025; down 40% from 99B pieces in FY2008 (christopholivierconsulting.com citing USPS OIG data)
USPS OIG (2025): projects 14-41% further decline in combined mail volume 2025-2035 in baseline scenario
piworld.com State of M&A Market Commercial Printing: 'PE involvement in general commercial printing remains limited' — reflecting the structural headwinds

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct = 0.15 (15% of direct mail firms materially using AI in automatable functions, defined as AI for data/list management, creative generation, or back-office automation at a material level; anchored by: large operators like Mittera and RRD are clearly AI-enabled, but the 924 firms with <5 employees are mostly small lettershops with minimal technology investment; 15% estimate reflects roughly 250 of 1,652 total firms being materially AI-enabled). historical_analog = 15 years for digital print technology adoption in commercial printing (offset-to-digital transition roughly 2000-2015 to reach majority adoption). Derivation: logistic curve from 0 to 50% over 15 years; at 15% current, on the lower-left of the S-curve; midpoint (~50%) is approximately 8-10 years away depending on rate of acceleration from AI-native competitors (e.g., programmatic direct mail platforms like Lob). Small lettershops are slow adopters (thin margins, limited capital, aging ownership). Setting t50 = 8.0 years (2026 to 2034), slightly more conservative than OOH given worse financial profile.

current_adoption_pct.source: PRINTING United Alliance / industry commentary; The Target Report (November 2024); Mittera company profile
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 25 print M&A transactions in November 2024 alone; Mittera at 24 acquisitions (technology-forward operator); 'direct mail printing remains a bullish segment for highly personalized, data-driven mail pieces on production inkjet.' AI/data adoption concentrated in larger operators and specialized platforms; small lettershops estimated at ~15% material AI adoption.

historical_analogs.value: 15 years (offset-to-digital print transition ~2000-2015; data/list management automation ~2005-2020)
historical_analogs.source: Industry commentary via piworld.com and printing industry sources
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Commercial printing companies that adapted to digital survived; those that did not closed. Technology transitions in printing typically take 10-15 years to achieve majority adoption across the fragmented industry — analog to digital; offset to inkjet.

owners_60plus_pct.source: Graphic Arts Advisors (via piworld.com) / Project Equity business-owner demographics
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Graphic Arts Advisors: succession planning challenge in commercial printing is 'almost ubiquitous' with owners wanting to retire with 'no succession plan within their business, no family members, nobody within the company to take it over.' Project Equity: 52.3% of US business owners are 55+; commercial printing skews older than average — ESTIMATE 60% of direct mail house owners are 60+ based on documented severity of aging-owner problem.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Graphic Arts Advisors senior managing director Mark Hahn: lack of succession planning in commercial printing is 'almost ubiquitous in that segment of the market'; owners 'wanting to retire with no succession plan within their business, no family members, nobody within the company to take it over' and exhibit 'a certain amount of owner fatigue' (piworld.com State of M&A Market for Commercial Printing). This directly documents a succession shortage in the commercial print/direct mail segment.

active_consolidators.source: The Target Report (November 2024) / Mittera company profile / piworld.com M&A analysis
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: 25 print M&A transactions in November 2024; Mittera identified as major consolidator (24 acquisitions as of October 2024); RRD (backed by Chatham Asset Management) active in direct mail; Mittera and Vericast/related platforms identified as active roll-up buyers. Count of 3 reflects: Mittera (active direct mail acquirer), RRD/Chatham (large-scale direct mail platform), and one additional active regional/PE-backed consolidator.

buy_mult.source: Peak Business Valuation (printing business valuation multiples) / ctacquisitions.com (2026 printing valuation) / Rock LaManna commentary
buy_mult.figure_quoted: EBITDA multiples for printing businesses usually fall between 2.99x and 3.93x EBITDA (Peak Business Valuation); Rock LaManna: 'the multiple for the printing industry is 4'; ctacquisitions.com: general commercial offset 3.5-4.5x, direct mail/variable-data hybrid 4.5-5.5x. For small direct mail operators (under $5M EBITDA, limited marketing services pull-through), buy_mult = 4.0x after normalization per rule 3 (SDE to EBITDA conversion: removing owner comp and adding market-rate management salary reduces effective multiple slightly from SDE basis). 4.0x is median of 3.0-5.5x range for small shops.

exit_mult.source: Marketing services M&A comps / ctacquisitions.com printing valuation 2026
exit_mult.figure_quoted: ctacquisitions.com: direct mail/variable-data with strong recurring marketing-services pull-through trades 5.5-6.5x EBITDA; label/packaging specialists 5-7x EBITDA; PE middle-market average 7.2-7.5x EBITDA (GF Data). Exit multiple for $10M+ EBITDA direct mail platform with marketing-services integration estimated at 7.0x — above the sub-scale operator range but below the branded marketing-services premium. Mittera (the main consolidator) likely exits at 6-8x as a technology-forward print/direct mail platform.

pricing_structure.value: Per-piece pricing with USPS postage passed through at cost. Competitive sealed-bid environment for mid-size jobs; direct pricing for recurring accounts. Postage is 50%+ of a direct mail campaign cost. Creative, data prep, and admin are value-added services that can be bundled or unbundled. No cost-plus protection — competitive bidding forces price transparency on all non-postage cost lines.
pricing_structure.source: USPS FY2025 Annual Report; Pragmatic CRM postage analysis 2025; industry commentary
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Marketing Mail revenue increased $350 million or 2.3% on a volume decline of 764 million pieces or 1.3% — revenue growing only due to rate increases; 7.38% average Marketing Mail rate increase effective July 2025; operator absorbs pass-through cost increase with zero capture leverage

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = V_raw × 100 = 0.106666 × 100 = 10.67 percentage points of revenue freed as labor savings. A = 1.25 (well below 5). No tension: the rule flags uplift_pp < 3 while A >= 5; here A < 5 so no conflict. The 10.67pp uplift is material but both adoption is slow (t50=8 years) and capture is low (C=0.75 due to deflation channel). Consistent — the low C score reflects that AI efficiency mostly flows to clients via competitive bidding, which is appropriate given the per-piece bid pricing model.

terminal_value.justification: Direct mail operators face a dual threat that warrants MELTING classification. First, demand melt: USPS Marketing Mail volume declined 40% from 99 billion pieces in FY2008 to 56.8 billion pieces in FY2025, a structural trend driven by electronic diversion. The USPS OIG projects a further 14-41% decline in combined mail volumes from 2025-2035. USPS rate increases (7.38% average in July 2025) compound the melt by making direct mail more expensive relative to digital alternatives. Second, AI accelerates the substitutes: AI simultaneously makes email, digital display, and programmatic advertising cheaper and better-targeted, specifically attacking the measurement and personalization advantages that made direct mail defensible. The operator's AI story is a cost cut on a melting revenue base — and the secular melt rate (3-5% per year volume decline) exceeds any plausible margin gain from AI efficiency in the white-collar cost lines. Classification: MELTING — the product base itself (physical direct mail volume) is being replaced by digital substitutes accelerated by AI capabilities.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from the automatable fraction of each OEWS occupation in the role_mix, applied to what current AI can substantially do in a direct mail house context — no reference to deal activity, consolidation trends, or M&A volumes. A was derived from current_adoption_pct estimated from operator technology profiles and the historical analog of digital technology adoption in commercial printing — neither involves PE deal flow or consolidation evidence. Confirmed: V and A justified without reference to deal activity.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Direct mail is explicitly a per-piece competitive-bid pricing model with postage passed through at cost. This is the paradigmatic deflation channel: AI savings in data prep, creative, and admin are exactly the lines that show up as cost reductions that competitors will match in the next bid, passing savings to clients as lower per-piece prices rather than retaining them as margin. pi_dist = 0.30 reflects this explicitly: the firm does not control the client (agency intermediation), the pricing model is fully transparent (bid-based), and AI bypasses the white-collar value-added layer that represented the firm's differentiation. pi_moat = 0.25 confirms no structural barrier prevents this deflation from fully flowing through. The 10.67pp uplift estimate is therefore largely theoretical from the firm's perspective — most of that labor saving will be competed away in lower per-piece bids within 2-3 years of widespread AI adoption among competing mail houses.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=6: A=1.667, S increases from ~2.43 to ~2.61. At t50=12: A=0.833, S decreases to ~2.18. Moderate sensitivity — A is already low so variation has limited impact on S. Range ~2.18-2.61 bracketing the base 2.43.

At pi_dist=0.20: C=0.50, S~2.16. At pi_dist=0.40: C=1.00, S~2.73. Most sensitive dimension — captures the central uncertainty of whether competitive bidding fully deflates AI savings. Range ~2.16-2.73.

At buy=3.0, exit=7.0: M=clamp(4×4/3,0,10)=clamp(5.333,0,10)=5.333; S~2.85. At buy=5.5, exit=7.0: M=clamp(4×1.5/5.5,0,10)=clamp(1.091,0,10)=1.091; S~2.10. M is highly sensitive to buy multiple — the low absolute multiple spread ($7-$4=$3) is compressed at the small end of the printing industry.

evidence.reviewer_note: Three structural concerns require reviewer attention. (1) n_band likely overstated: the dataset uses the generic Business & Consumer Services EBITDA margin of 15.65%. Actual print/mail EBITDA margins are typically 5-10% for small shops; at 7.5% margin, the 20-99 class ($12M avg revenue → $0.9M EBITDA) would fall OUT of the $1-10M band, and the 100-499 class ($31M avg → $2.3M EBITDA) would remain in-band. Under a 7.5% margin, n_band would drop to ~62 (100-499 class only), dramatically reducing TD and B. The prompt instructs to use the provided dataset value (261) and flag. (2) Role-mix contamination (same as 541850): the OEWS proxy is 4-digit 5418 dominated by ad agencies. Actual direct mail houses are production plants — press operators (51-5112), mail machine operators (43-9051), insertion crews — none appearing in the top-10. The unlisted 49.3% bucket captures these physical workers; re-weighting sets 35% of total wage bill to production (0% automatable). The low labor_share of 0.2807 corroborates this materials/postage-heavy production character. (3) MELTING terminal value creates a systemic risk to M: the secular volume decline in Marketing Mail (down 40% since 2008, projected further decline) means that roll-up exit multiples may compress over time as the medium shrinks, making the buy-mult/exit-mult spread potentially negative in a 5-7 year hold. The static multiple spread used here (buy=4x, exit=7x) assumes a functioning roll-up exit market exists; if USPS volume continues to melt and PE interest in the sector fades, M would be lower.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: In 541870, these are route salespeople selling coverage to local advertisers. AI can automate quoting, coverage-area reporting, and proposals; the client relationship with local merchants and CPG reps persists as the distributor's commercial core.

Agency-contamination role in the 4-digit proxy; virtually absent in real 541870 distribution firms. To the extent any exist (distribution coordination, client communications), drafting and reporting are AI-automatable; judgment and coordination persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists (13-1161)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: In 541870, this maps to route targeting, carrier-route geo-selection, coverage mapping and delivery verification reporting. These functions are already largely software-driven (GIS tools, postal carrier-route databases); generative AI and route-optimization algorithms can automate the remaining analytical work nearly fully.

Crew scheduling, GPS-based delivery verification, and payroll processing automate well; managing a high-churn gig workforce, resolving field exceptions, and client relationship management remain human. 25% automatable reflects the ops-reporting and admin scheduling slice.

Client flyer/insert layout and production design are substantially automatable by AI generative design tools. The creative judgment and brand-specific direction component is smaller in commodity-print production for local advertisers.

Physical in-store sampling and product demonstration are not automatable. AI can assist with scheduling and route assignment (the 5% fraction), but the in-person staffing and performance are physical presence work.
Similar to advertising sales agents; proposals, coverage reporting and renewal paperwork automate; relationship maintenance with local clients persists.
Physical in-store placement and rack-stocking are not automatable. The negligible 5% covers scheduling and planogram reporting.

Agency contamination in the 4-digit proxy; in real 541870 firms this is a targeting/program management function. Campaign reporting, coverage analytics and client program tracking automate; supplier management and key-account stewardship persist.
In commodity-print context for local advertisers, art direction is substantially templated. AI tools can generate layouts and format inserts; creative strategy and final brand approval persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unlisted remainder — field distribution labor (walkers, drivers, rack stockers) and back-office admin
breakdown_by_role.rationale: The listed top-10 wage shares sum to 0.507, leaving 0.493 in unlisted occupations. Per industry guidance, the real 541870 workforce is dominated by field distribution labor (walkers, drivers, rack stockers — ~40% of total wage bill) which is not automatable (physical), plus back-office admin (~9% of total wage bill) which is largely automatable (billing, gig worker onboarding, client reporting). Weighted fraction: ((0.40 × 0.0) + (0.093 × 0.85)) / 0.493 = 0.07905/0.493 = 0.1603, rounded to 0.15 to reflect that some of the residual is also lightly automatable supervisory work. Net within-role fraction = 0.15.

pi_dist.rationale: Clients (local advertisers, CPG brands, national retailers) treat physical coverage as a commodity purchased on per-piece price. USPS EDDM at $0.242-$0.291/piece sets the hard pricing ceiling for private distributors. No long-term contracts are typical; clients re-bid annually or switch to digital channels. When AI reduces the firm's routing and admin costs, price competition from USPS and competing crews means savings pass through as lower rates rather than accruing to the operator. Client-relationship control is low: the buyer is the advertising spend decision-maker, not the distribution firm.
sources: USPS Every Door Direct Mail pricing: https://www.usps.com/business/every-door-direct-mail.htm (EDDM BMEU DDU $0.242, Retail $0.26 per piece, accessed 2026-07-20)
mailpro.org EDDM Cost guide 2026: https://www.mailpro.org/post/eddm-cost/ (rate schedule $0.242-$0.291 per piece)

pi_moat.rationale: Barriers to entry and switching costs are low. No licensure required. Physical route coverage is the only real differentiator; USPS EDDM replicates it at commodity price. GPS/photo proof-of-delivery tech is now commoditized, eroding any premium for verified delivery. The primary AI bypass threat is not AI doing physical distribution (impossible), but advertisers using AI-optimized digital channels (programmatic, social, search) to abandon physical advertising entirely, threatening total addressable demand. Damodaran proxy margin of 15.65% likely overstates actual margins for a declining-volume, labor-intensive business.
sources: Swiftly blog, The Decline of Print Circulars: https://www.swiftly.com/blog/the-decline-of-print-circulars-what-retailers-need-to-do-to-stay-relevant (digital solutions provide 50-75% cost reduction vs print, accessed 2026-07-20)

Winterberry Group Dec 2024: https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2024/12/11/2995426/0/en/Winterberry-Group-Research-Examines-Direct-Mail-Ad-Spend-Demand-Drivers.html (direct mail spend grew 2.6% in 2024 but follows double-digit decline in 2023, accessed 2026-07-20)

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct = 0.10 (10% of 541870 firms materially use AI in automatable functions — routing, admin, design). Historical analog: GPS/route optimization software took approximately 8-10 years from commercial introduction (~2005) to 50% penetration among US delivery/distribution SMBs (~2013-2015). AI tools are more accessible (SaaS, no capital investment), partially offsetting small-firm adoption lag. Derivation: analog midpoint 9 years × small-firm lag factor 1.1 × AI accessibility acceleration 0.8 = 7.9 years. Starting from 10% adoption at mid-2026, to reach 50% requires passing 40pp in ~7 years (by 2033), consistent with the adjusted analog. t50 = 7.0 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: ESTIMATE — basis: general SMB AI adoption surveys (2024) show ~15-20% of US SMBs materially use AI tools; physical distribution businesses with low-tech field labor and informal gig structure expected below average; 541870 specific: route optimization already largely baked into larger firms (pre-AI software), back-office AI still nascent for <20-employee distribution crews
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: ESTIMATE: ~10% of 541870 firms materially using AI in automatable functions (routing, admin, design) as of mid-2026

historical_analogs.value: 8-10 years for GPS/route optimization to reach 50% adoption among US SMB delivery/distribution operators (introduced ~2005, widespread ~2013-2015); 7-9 years for digital billing/ERP adoption among small service firms
historical_analogs.source: ESTIMATE — based on general technology diffusion patterns for SMB logistics/distribution sector; no primary source found specifically for 541870 technology adoption timeline
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: ESTIMATE: 8-10 year analog for prior comparable technology adoption in physical distribution/delivery sector

owners_60plus_pct.source: U.S. Census Bureau / Gallup Pathways to Wealth Survey 2024 (cross-industry proxy) — 52.3% of US employer-businesses owned by those 55+; Boomers (60-78) = 37% of small business owners; adjusted upward for 541870's sunset-industry characteristics (high proportion of <5-employee informal operators, likely older, less likely to have successors in a declining sector)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: just over half (52.3%) of U.S. employer-businesses are owned by people who are at or near retirement age, those 55 and older; Boomers (60-78) make up 37% of small business owners (Gallup Pathways to Wealth Survey, fall 2024 sample)

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Gallup 2024: roughly 50% of all small business owners either plan to close their business or lack any succession plan; 70% of small businesses fail to sell at time of owner retirement (Teamshares succession planning statistics). For 541870 specifically, the secular decline of print advertising distribution (Vericast/Valassis distress; P&G brandSAVER ceased FSI distribution 2023; print ad spend fell double-digits in 2023) makes succession even harder — buyers for a melting-medium distribution business are scarce.

active_consolidators.source: Web search for PE-backed roll-up platforms in physical flyer/circular/advertising material distribution, 2023-2025 — none found. The sector is in secular decline (print circular volumes falling, Vericast/Valassis sold to RRD after distress, no active consolidation identified). The Vericast-to-RRD transaction was a distressed sale, not a PE roll-up platform.
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: No PE-backed platforms identified actively doing add-on acquisitions in NAICS 541870 in the last 24 months. Vericast sold digital and print marketing businesses (including shared-mail/Valassis) to RRD in Q2 2024 for ~$1.3B — a strategic sale following Vericast's debt restructuring, not a roll-up consolidator.

buy_mult.source: ESTIMATE — basis: BizBuySell 2024 median SDE multiple 2.57x across all small businesses; marketing/advertising services BizBuySell SDE ~3.0-3.5x per industry benchmarks; First Page Sage 2025 marketing agency EBITDA multiples $1-3M EBITDA range 4.5-5.5x but those are agency businesses (recurring clients, better margins). Physical distribution businesses in secular demand decline apply a structural discount. SDE-to-EBITDA conversion: small distribution firm owner is working in the business, SDE ≈ EBITDA + $150-200K owner comp; at $500K-$1M SDE and 3.0x SDE → EBITDA ~$350-800K, EBITDA multiple ≈ 3.5-4.5x. Given secular decline, lower end of range. Estimate: 3.5x EBITDA.
buy_mult.figure_quoted: ESTIMATE: 3.5x EBITDA for small 541870 distribution firms. Basis: BizBuySell 2024 median SDE 2.57x all businesses; marketing/ad services 3.0-3.5x SDE; converting SDE to EBITDA for working-owner small distribution firm yields ~3.5x EBITDA before secular-decline discount.

exit_mult.source: ESTIMATE — basis: Packaging/logistics PE deal median EV/EBITDA ~5.8x in 2024 (M&A market data); marketing services platform exits $5-10M EBITDA range ~6-7x; the Vericast/Valassis sale to RRD (Q2 2024, ~$1.3B) was a distressed strategic deal not reflective of platform PE exit pricing; print/mail distribution platforms command lower multiples than digital agencies due to secular demand decline. Estimate: 6.0x EBITDA for a $10M+ EBITDA distribution platform.
exit_mult.figure_quoted: ESTIMATE: 6.0x EBITDA exit multiple for 541870 platform. Basis: packaging/logistics PE deals median ~5.8x in 2024; marketing services mid-market ~6-7x; print distribution discount vs. digital offset by physical-network moat.

pricing_structure.value: Per-piece / per-thousand billing (~85-90% of revenue); targeting/design add-ons (~10-15% of revenue). USPS EDDM sets the pricing ceiling at $0.242-$0.291/piece at bulk mail; private door-to-door distribution charges $0.25-$0.45/piece depending on density and verification requirements. Commodity per-unit pricing with no retainer or recurring-contract structure.
pricing_structure.source: USPS EDDM rate schedule; mailpro.org EDDM cost guide 2026; Valpak FDD 2025 ($0.168/insert fee for franchise marketing); market pricing observations from MarketAnywhere flyer distribution service
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: EDDM BMEU DDU rate $0.242/piece; EDDM Retail $0.26/piece; Valpak franchise marketing fee $0.168/insert. Private distribution typically $0.25-$0.45/piece door-to-door.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 11.0174 (V_raw × 100 = 11.02pp of revenue freed). A = 1.43, well below 5. Cross-check condition (uplift < 3pp while A >= 5) does NOT trigger. The modest A reflects slow adoption in a small-firm, physical-labor business; the relatively high uplift reflects the significant admin/routing/design automation potential. Consistent: a slow-adopting industry (A < 2) with moderate AI value (V ~4.4) is internally coherent.

terminal_value.justification: The product being distributed — printed advertising materials (circulars, FSIs, coupons, flyers, samples) — is in secular structural decline. FSI volumes fell 15% in 2020; digital coupons first exceeded FSI redemptions in 2020; P&G brandSAVER ceased all FSI distribution in 2023; US print advertising declined >30% since 2020; direct mail spend fell double digits in 2023. AI accelerates the melt by making digital substitutes (AI-optimized programmatic ads, personalized digital coupons) cheaper and better-targeted per response dollar. The distributor's physical delivery labor survives automation, but the demand for what they deliver is the melting element. USPS EDDM pricing ($0.242-$0.291/piece) competes with free or near-free digital alternatives for the same advertising outcome. This is a cost-cutting story on a structurally melting medium — the melt dominates the terminal value assessment.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was determined solely by computing AI-automatable fractions of the role mix (routing/geo-targeting at 80%, graphic design at 65%, admin/back-office at ~85%) applied to a physical-labor workforce structure. No deal activity or consolidation trends were referenced. A was derived from current adoption estimate (10%) and historical analog of GPS/route optimization software penetration in SMB distribution (8-10 year adoption cycle). No M&A or PE consolidation data used for V or A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing is per-piece/per-thousand ($0.242-$0.45/piece depending on method). USPS EDDM sets the hard pricing ceiling. This is a commodity price-per-unit market with no retainer, no cost-plus billing, and no mechanism for the operator to retain AI savings as margin: any reduction in the firm's routing, admin or design costs is immediately competed away through lower per-piece bids when clients re-evaluate annually. This dynamic is reflected in pi_dist = 0.30 (low: clients control pricing anchor) and pi_moat = 0.25 (low: USPS substitute, no switching costs, commoditized delivery verification).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=5: A=2.0, S≈2.83. At t50=10: A=1.0, S≈2.25. Range: S from ~2.25 to ~2.83. t50 is the dominant uncertainty in A but given the low C score (0.75), even faster adoption does not dramatically change the composite.

At buy=2.5, exit=6.0: M=clamp(4×(6-2.5)/2.5,0,10)=clamp(5.6,0,10)=5.6, S≈2.94. At buy=4.5, exit=6.0: M=clamp(4×(6-4.5)/4.5,0,10)=clamp(1.33,0,10)=1.33, S≈2.05. Wide range; secular decline creates buyer reluctance that could compress buy_mult further.

At 0.35: OW_raw=(0.35-0.10)/0.35=0.714, +0.1=0.814; B=10×sqrt(0.502150×0.814)/1=10×0.639=6.39, S≈2.39. At 0.55: OW_raw=clamp(1.286,0.1,0.9)=0.9, +0.1=1.0 (same as base); no change from base. Floor dominates: impact is one-directional downward.

evidence.reviewer_note: Role-mix contamination is severe: the 4-digit 5418 proxy is dominated by ad-agency occupations (PR specialists 8.3%, graphic designers 3.3%, art directors 4.1%, marketing managers 5.2%) that essentially do not exist in real 541870 distribution firms. The real wage bill is dominated by unlisted field-distribution labor (walkers, drivers, rack stockers estimated at ~40% of total wage bill). The 'unlisted remainder' bucket (0.493 wage share) captures this with a blended 0.15 fraction. The n_band quirk noted in the prompt (500+ employee class still inside the $1-10M band at the 15.65% Damodaran margin) is realistic — real margins in a labor-heavy, secular-decline distribution business are likely below 15.65%, which would reduce n_band further and lower B; this is a downside risk. No active PE consolidators found, consistent with the industry's structural challenges. Boundary note: USPS direct mail (541860), shared-mail franchisors (541860/541870 straddle), and in-store demo/merchandising (541890/561990) have overlapping economics — this record scores the physical door-to-door/insertion distribution core. The Vericast/Valassis-to-RRD transaction (2024) is treated as a distressed strategic sale, not a PE roll-up exit, and is therefore not used for exit_mult; the 6.0x estimate is based on comparable packaging/logistics PE comps.

breakdown_by_role.role: Advertising Sales Agents (41-3011) — promo products account managers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: In the dominant sub-segment (promo products distribution), this role covers inside sales and account management. AI already automates product search, supplier sourcing, quoting, virtual mockup generation, and order paperwork — the core transactional workflow. What persists: the client relationship with corporate procurement buyers, upselling, event/program strategy. 45% reflects the substantial automation of the sourcing-to-quote workflow while preserving the relationship-management component.

breakdown_by_role.role: Public Relations Specialists (27-3031) — agency contamination; program comms in promo
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Agency contamination from the 4-digit proxy; minimally present in real 541890 promo-products firms. Where present (program reporting, campaign recaps, client communications), AI substantially automates drafting and summarization. Creative judgment and relationship management persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists (13-1161) — product/trend research
breakdown_by_role.rationale: In promo products context, this function covers product search, supplier discovery, category trend analysis, and program research — all near-fully AI-automatable. ASI/SAGE database search is already software; AI generative tools can source product alternatives, prepare trend briefs, and compile supplier comparisons with minimal human input. Residual 25% = client-category strategy interpretation.

breakdown_by_role.role: General and Operations Managers (11-1021) — distributor principals and ops managers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Supplier negotiation and key-account stewardship are human; ops reporting, inventory/program admin, and routine billing oversight automate. 30% reflects the admin/reporting portion of a working distributor-principal's week.

breakdown_by_role.role: Graphic Designers (27-1024) — imprint proofs, virtual mockups, spec sheets
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Logo placement, imprint proofs, virtual mockups and spec sheets are ALREADY the leading AI use case in promo products — AI design tools generate them in seconds. PPAI/ASI surveys report 78% of distributors using AI for marketing content including artwork. The residual 15% is custom or complex creative requiring human art direction.

breakdown_by_role.role: Demonstrators and Product Promoters (41-9011) — in-store sampling/demo staff
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical in-store demonstration and sampling staffing is not automatable — it is presence work. The 5% fraction captures scheduling and route/event assignment automation only.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services (41-3091) — outside sales and account reps
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Similar to advertising sales agents: proposal automation, CRM data entry, order status updates, and follow-up communications automate well. The relationship-management and consultative-selling component — the distributor's primary source of switching cost — persists.

breakdown_by_role.role: Merchandise Displayers and Window Trimmers (27-1026) — physical display work
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical display and rack setup is not automatable. The 5% captures planogram reporting and digital visual-merchandising planning.

breakdown_by_role.role: Marketing Managers (11-2021) — program marketing and merch-store management
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Campaign reporting, program recaps, company merch-store management and client analytics automate substantially. Strategy, supplier relationship management, and key-account business development persist. PPAI research: 88% of PPAI 100 distributors use AI for marketing purposes.

breakdown_by_role.role: Art Directors (27-1011) — creative direction for promo programs
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Virtual proof creation, template artwork generation and branded merchandise visual styling are largely AI-automatable. Creative strategy, client-brand interpretation and final approval workflows remain human. 55% reflects the heavy template/proof production component.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unlisted remainder — order operations/fulfillment coordination, field/event staff, admin
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Listed top-10 wage shares sum to 0.507, leaving 0.493 in unlisted occupations. Per prompt guidance for 541890, this includes: order operations/fulfillment coordination and admin (~25-35% of total wage bill — PO entry, supplier chasing, proof routing, billing automate near-fully; physical kitting/fulfillment doesn't); field/event staff (~10-15% of total wage bill — physical presence, not automatable); and other residual. Blended fraction: estimated 30% of total wage bill is automatable order-ops/admin (fraction 0.80), 13% is field/event (fraction 0.05), and 10.3% is other supervision/misc (fraction 0.25). Weighted: ((0.30×0.80)+(0.13×0.05)+(0.063×0.25))/0.493 = (0.240+0.0065+0.01575)/0.493 = 0.26225/0.493 = 0.5320. Adjusted downward to 0.35 to account for the catch-all heterogeneity — the demo/sampling and experiential sub-segments within the unlisted tail have much lower automatable fractions, diluting the estimate for the whole 541890 code.

pi_dist.rationale: Promotional products distributors price as a markup over supplier cost (industry-standard 30-40% gross margins per ASI benchmark data). Unlike cost-plus or audited billing, the markup is generally not visible to clients, giving distributors partial pricing control. However: (1) AI-native direct platforms (4imprint at $1.37B revenue with 10.8% operating margin as largest distributor) and print-on-demand services price near cost for routine orders, creating a market ceiling for independent distributor pricing; (2) corporate procurement increasingly uses RFPs and reverse auctions that reduce markup; (3) sales reps own client relationships and can move books between distributorships (portability risk). Partial control: pi_dist = 0.35.
sources: 4imprint 2024 annual results (Wikipedia): revenue $1,367.9M, operating income $148.1M, operating margin 10.8% — largest distributor benchmark for platform pricing dynamics (accessed 2026-07-20)

PPAI 2024 industry data: $26.78B total US distributor sales, large distributors 54% of sales (accessed 2026-07-20): https://www.ppai.org/media-hub/ppai-sales-volume-estimate-modest-growth-in-2024-but-reasons-for-optimism-remain/
PPAI ESTIMATE on gross margins: industry standard 30-40% gross margin for independent distributors (basis: consistent industry reporting, multiple ASI/PPAI sources)

pi_moat.rationale: Barriers to entry and switching costs are moderate-low. Key-person portability is the dominant risk: sales reps own the account book and routinely move between distributorships — a roll-up acquires revenue that may walk with the rep. ASI/SAGE membership gates for supplier access are weakening (more suppliers accept direct corporate orders). Virtual mockup/proof generation is now near-commodity via AI. Physical kitting/fulfillment provides a moderate moat for distributors holding inventory. The primary bypass threat is AI-native platforms and print-on-demand eliminating the independent distributor for routine branded merch orders. No licensure; switching is low-friction for clients who find a competing distributor.
sources: PPAI research: 59% of PPAI 100 distributors use AI — vanguard adoption creates platform competition for smaller independent distributors (accessed 2026-07-20): https://www.ppai.org/media-hub/ai-s-role-in-promo-grows-despite-hurdles/

Champlain Capital / Align Capital iPROMOTEu transaction Jan 2023 — PE actively consolidating to capture scale against platform competition: https://aligncp.com/news/align-capital-partners-completes-sale-of-ipromoteu/ (accessed 2026-07-20)

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct = 0.25 (25% of all 541890 firms materially using AI in automatable functions — product search, artwork proofs, order entry, marketing content). PPAI 100 vanguard already at 59%; the micro-distributor tail (<5 employees = 72% of firms by count) is well below. Historical analog: ASI/SAGE online catalog/ordering platform adoption — from ~2003-2005 industry adoption to ~50% penetration by ~2010-2012 = approximately 7-8 year window. AI tools are more accessible (SaaS, low or no capital cost). Starting from 25% at mid-2026, to reach 50% requires adding 25pp across all firm sizes, most of the distance already covered in the vanguard (PPAI 100 at 59%). Estimated 6 years (by 2032) for industry-wide 50% threshold. Derivation: analog 7.5 years × (1 - 0.25/0.50) adjustment for already-started adoption × AI accessibility factor 0.90 = 7.5 × 0.5 × 0.90 = 3.4 — too fast; correction: the 25% current adoption is concentrated in the top tier, the micro-tail is <5%, so the diffusion from 25% industry-average to 50% industry-average still requires the full analog timeline minus elapsed. Revised: 6.0 years from today (2026).

current_adoption_pct.source: PPAI research — AI adoption in promotional products industry; PPAI 100 distributors (top 100 by revenue) at 59%; full industry including micro-distributors estimated at 25%
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 59% of PPAI 100 distributors use AI in their business operations; 78% of PPAI 100 distributors use AI for marketing content generation (PPAI Research, 2024/2025). Full industry estimate: 25% materially using AI across all 6,397 firms, including the micro-distributor tail where AI adoption is low.

historical_analogs.value: 7-8 years for ASI/SAGE online catalog and e-ordering platform adoption to reach 50% of promotional products distributors (~2003-2010); 8-10 years for digital order management/ERP adoption among small distributors
historical_analogs.source: ESTIMATE — based on ASI annual technology surveys (historical context) and general SMB technology diffusion patterns in specialty distribution; no single primary source with exact year-by-year adoption curve for this specific industry
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: ESTIMATE: ASI/SAGE online ordering reached approximately 50% distributor adoption within ~7-8 years of widespread platform availability (~2003-2010). AI adoption is expected to diffuse at a similar or faster rate given SaaS accessibility and lower capital barriers.

owners_60plus_pct.source: U.S. Census Bureau / Gallup Pathways to Wealth Survey 2024 (cross-industry proxy) — 52.3% of US employer-businesses owned by those 55+; Boomers (60-78) = 37% of small business owners; 4,615 of 6,397 firms have <5 employees (72%), consistent with founder-operator model skewing older
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: just over half (52.3%) of U.S. employer-businesses are owned by people who are at or near retirement age, those 55 and older; Boomers (60-78) make up 37% of small business owners (Gallup Pathways to Wealth Survey, fall 2024)

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Gallup 2024 survey: roughly 50% of all small business owners either plan to close their business or lack any succession plan; 70% of small businesses fail to sell at time of owner retirement (Teamshares succession statistics). For promo products specifically: the industry has >6,000 distributors most of which are solo/micro shops; PE consolidators (HALO/TPG Growth, iPROMOTEu/Champlain Capital) are explicitly targeting the succession opportunity to acquire founder-operator books of business at retirement.

active_consolidators.source: Web research: identified 3 active PE-backed platforms doing add-on acquisitions in 541890 in the last 24 months
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: 1) HALO Branded Solutions (TPG Growth, acquired Aug 2023) — active M&A strategy, second largest US distributor with history of acquiring Counselor Top 40 firms; 2) iPROMOTEu (Champlain Capital, acquired Jan 2023) — acquired AIA Corporation in 2024, ranked #8 distributor in 2025 Counselor Top 40; 3) Advantage Solutions (public, ADSY) — active consolidation in demo/sampling/field-marketing sub-segment within 541890. Count = 3 confirmed PE-backed or strategic consolidation platforms actively doing add-ons.

buy_mult.source: ESTIMATE — basis: First Page Sage 2025: $1-3M EBITDA marketing agencies 4.5-5.5x; BizBuySell 2024 wholesale/distribution at $3M sales ~3.4x SDE; promotional products distributors score between these benchmarks. Key-person portability risk (sales reps own account books and can move to competing distributors) applies a discount vs. pure agency multiples. EBITDA normalization: commission-only reps and owner-operator compensation require careful SDE-to-EBITDA conversion — at the $1-3M EBITDA level, estimate EBITDA multiple of 4.5x on a normalized post-owner-comp basis.
buy_mult.figure_quoted: ESTIMATE: 4.5x EBITDA for small 541890 promo-products distributor ($1-3M EBITDA). Basis: marketing agencies $1-3M EBITDA at 4.5-5.5x (First Page Sage 2025); distribution discount for key-person risk → 4.5x.

exit_mult.source: ESTIMATE — basis: PE platform exits in promo products distribution: HALO Branded Solutions sold to TPG Growth (Aug 2023) and iPROMOTEu sold to Champlain Capital (Jan 2023) — both at undisclosed terms but consistent with marketing services platform range. Marketing services platforms at $10M+ EBITDA: 7-9x based on sector M&A benchmarks. Advantage Solutions (public proxy for field marketing/sampling platform) trades at ~7-8x adjusted EBITDA ($356M adj EBITDA on $3.57B revenue). Using midpoint 8.0x for a promo distribution platform with $10M+ EBITDA.
exit_mult.figure_quoted: ESTIMATE: 8.0x EBITDA exit multiple for 541890 platform. Basis: HALO/TPG Growth (Aug 2023) and iPROMOTEu/Champlain Capital (Jan 2023) — terms undisclosed; Advantage Solutions market comparable ~7-8x adj EBITDA (2024 adj EBITDA $356M; market cap ~$700M = ~2x, but includes debt load from LBO); marketing services platform sector 7-9x. Midpoint: 8.0x.

pricing_structure.value: Markup over supplier cost — independent distributors apply 30-40% gross margin to supplier invoice price. No retainer or subscription model for most small distributors; billing is per-order. Commission-only sales reps typically receive 50%+ of gross margin dollars. Platform distributors (4imprint) operate direct/online with lower gross margins but higher volume. Program/corporate clients may have annual contracts with approved-supplier pricing.
pricing_structure.source: PPAI 2024 industry data; 4imprint 2024 annual results (Wikipedia); ASI industry benchmarks
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: US distributor promotional products sales $26.78B in 2024 (2.63% growth). 4imprint operating income $148.1M on $1,367.9M revenue = 10.8% operating margin as platform benchmark. Independent distributors: 30-40% gross margin standard (ASI).

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 11.1491 (V_raw × 100 = 11.15pp of revenue freed). A = 1.667, well below 5. Cross-check condition (uplift < 3pp while A >= 5) does NOT trigger. The moderate A reflects the multi-year adoption timeline for micro-distributor tail even as the PPAI 100 vanguard already shows 59% adoption. The relatively high uplift (11.15pp) is consistent with a moderately labor-intensive business with significant automatable back-office and creative functions.

terminal_value.justification: The dominant sub-segment's product — physical branded promotional merchandise (branded mugs, apparel, pens, tote bags) — cannot be replaced by AI; you cannot digitize a branded mug. Total US promo industry sales grew to $26.8B in 2024 and $27.1B in 2025 (PPAI), confirming durable demand. However, AI attacks the distributor's intermediation function — product search, artwork/proofs, ordering — which is the independent distributor's core value-add. AI-native platforms (4imprint at $1.4B revenue, online merch stores, print-on-demand) are eroding the independent distributor's margin by offering self-service at lower cost. The deciding mechanism: if AI enables corporate buyers to design merch and order directly from suppliers or AI-native platforms, the independent distributor is disintermediated. Current evidence (PPAI data, platform growth) suggests demand for the product is DURABLE but the independent distributor's economics are PRESSURED by disintermediation. Terminal value class: PRESSURED.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was set by computing AI-automatable fractions of the dominant sub-segment (promo products distribution) workforce structure applied to the 4-digit role mix — specifically: artwork/proofs at 85%, product search/research at 75%, account management at 40-45%, order ops admin at 35% blended. PPAI survey data on AI adoption (59% PPAI 100 at 59% using AI for content/marketing) was used as evidence of adoption speed (A input), not as evidence for V. No deal activity, consolidation trends, or M&A data used for V or A inputs.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing is per-order markup over supplier cost (30-40% gross margins for independent distributors). This is not cost-plus or audited billing, so AI savings do not mechanically flow to clients — distributors can theoretically retain margin. However, AI-native platforms (4imprint, online merch sites) with self-service ordering apply competitive ceiling pressure: when AI enables clients to bypass the distributor entirely or to compare supplier prices directly, the independent distributor's markup is competed away. This dynamic is reflected in pi_dist = 0.35 (moderate-low control) and pi_moat = 0.30 (moderate-low barrier). The per-order pricing model itself does not create automatic pass-through, but platform competition does.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=4: A=2.5, S≈3.30. At t50=9: A=1.111, S≈2.56. Range of S: ~2.56 to ~3.30. The PPAI 100 at 59% adoption suggests the vanguard is already past 50%; the full-industry t50 depends on diffusion speed in the micro-distributor tail.

At buy=3.5, exit=8.0: M=clamp(4×(8.0-3.5)/3.5,0,10)=clamp(5.143,0,10)=5.143, S≈3.14. At buy=6.0, exit=8.0: M=clamp(4×(8.0-6.0)/6.0,0,10)=clamp(1.333,0,10)=1.333, S≈2.35. Key-person portability risk is the dominant uncertainty in buy_mult.

At ars=0.32: V_raw=0.2741×0.32=0.08771, V=10×(0.08771/0.25)=3.508, S≈2.65. At ars=0.50: V_raw=0.2741×0.50=0.13705, V=10×min(1,0.13705/0.25)=5.482, S≈3.01. The catch-all heterogeneity (demo/event sub-segments with lower AI fractions) and the unlisted remainder composition are the main sources of uncertainty.

evidence.reviewer_note: 541890 is a catch-all code. Dominant sub-segment scored: promotional products distribution (branded merchandise), consistent with PPAI data showing ~$26.8B US total distributor sales in 2024 vs. SUSB $19.4B receipts for the code (gap reflects PPAI including some suppliers/manufacturers; distributor-only segment is approximately $16-17B, plausibly the majority of 541890 receipts). Other sub-segments in this code: in-store demonstration/sampling services (physically non-automatable; pulls V down if larger than assumed — Advantage Solutions proxy segment shows 10% EBITDA margin); event/experiential marketing support; display lettering; aerial advertising. Role-mix contamination: the 4-digit 5418 proxy imports ad-agency occupations; re-weighted toward sales (accounts for 45% automatable fraction) and order-ops/admin (35% blended for unlisted remainder). Key-person portability is the dominant moat risk for the promo-products sub-segment and depresses pi_moat and buy_mult. The n_band is well-defined (482 firms in 20-499 employee classes with $1-10M EBITDA at Damodaran margin); the 500+ class exceeds the band (avg EBITDA $17.5M at 15.65% margin). EBITDA normalization note: promo distributors running on commission-only reps need gross vs. net revenue normalization before applying Damodaran margins — actual EBITDA margins for small distributors likely lower than 15.65%, which would reduce n_band and B; this is a downside risk. heterogeneous = true.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Questionnaire drafting, coding open-ends, cross-tab analysis, chart production and report writing are heavily AI-automated per GRIT 2025 (67% of suppliers embed GenAI in deliverables). Study design judgment, methodological defense and client counsel persist as human-required. Role share hypothetical per prompt guidance; OEWS 4-digit 5419 data is contaminated by veterinary occupations (vet techs 9.2%, vets 17.1% of wage bill) and is unusable.

Online panel sampling, survey programming and quality-control automate near-fully; CATI phone interviewing replaced by AI voice agents. In-person qual (focus groups, CLTs), ethnography and B2B expert recruitment stay human. Role share hypothetical per prompt guidance.

AI moderation of async qual and auto-summarization of transcripts is live; senior moderator-client trust and workshop facilitation persist and are personal to individual. Automation fraction is intentionally conservative because qualitative interpretation and workshop facilitation resist AI substitution.
Proposal drafting and RFP responses automate; account relationships with brand insights teams persist and are personal to founders/directors. Revenue often follows the individual, not the firm.

Project P&L tracking, resource scheduling and pipeline management automate. Client escalation, methodology governance and firm strategy persist. One uncontaminated OEWS signal: 11-1021 General & Operations Managers 6.6% of 5419 wage bill; scaling to 541910 suggests 8-10% is plausible.

Billing, incentive/honoraria processing, scheduling and compliance paperwork automate near-fully; consistent with all-industry admin automation benchmarks. Role share deliberately conservative at low end (7%) because small firms have thin admin layers.

pi_dist.rationale: Approximately 70% of revenue comes from fixed-price competitive project bids; when AI collapses programming/fieldwork/analysis costs, bid competition passes savings to clients as lower project prices (deflation channel active). Tracker/retainer revenue (~20-25%) is stickier but still re-biddable when AI-native competitors quote 50% less. Client relationship is partially personal to the senior researcher (founder-to-client), but large buyers run structured RFP processes where price competes. Pricing-model deflation is the dominant mechanism keeping pi_dist below 0.5.
sources: GreenBook GRIT 2025 Business Outlook: tech-led suppliers revenue health 98.2 vs service-led 34.0, indicating pricing pressure on traditional full-service firms
ESOMAR/GRIT commentary on AI-native entrants quoting dramatically lower prices

pi_moat.rationale: No licensure barrier. Switching costs exist for tracker studies (methodology continuity, trend breaks) and for long-standing brand clients, but DIY platforms (Qualtrics, SurveyMonkey) have been eroding the low end for 15 years — established base rate for AI disruption. AI-native entrants (Zappi $170M raise) and in-house insourcing are documented threats. Proprietary panel assets offer some moat for panel-owning firms; boutiques without panel assets have limited differentiation beyond methodology reputation. B2B expert recruitment and claims-substantiation/litigation survey work requires verifiably human samples, preserving moat in that niche.
sources: Zappi $170M raise (Crunchbase 2022) as evidence of AI-native competitive threat
GRIT 2025: 72% of buyers now use GenAI in at least one research stage (Perspective AI blog citing GRIT)
15-year DIY platform erosion precedent (Qualtrics, SurveyMonkey)

t50_years.derivation: GRIT 2025 data: 72% of insights buyers use GenAI in at least one research stage (up from 23% in 2023); 67% of suppliers embed GenAI directly in client deliverables. The 50% threshold was crossed in 2024-2025, placing t50 in the past. Setting t50 = 1.0 year reflects that we are approximately 1 year past the crossing of the 50% threshold for supplier-side material adoption. Historical analog: online panels displaced CATI over ~10 years (1995-2005); DIY survey platforms reached ~50% of small-study volume over ~8-10 years (2005-2015). AI adoption has moved dramatically faster — from 23% to 72% buyer adoption in two years (2023-2025), implying t50 has already been reached and 1.0 is a generous forward-looking floor.
t50_years.plausible_range: 0.5-3.0 years (0.5 if t50 already passed; 3.0 if 'material adoption' standard is higher than GRIT's broad definition)

AI adoption appears dramatically faster: 23% to 72% buyer adoption in two years (2023-2025 per GRIT).

Insights-industry specific: boutique founder-led firms dominate (2,509 of 3,747 firms <5 employees per SUSB 2022) and GRIT data shows individual senior researcher controls client relationship — departure typically means revenue loss rather than transfer. No Insights Association member demographic study found with age-specific figures; applying general small business 60+ estimate of ~40% as ESTIMATE.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 13.47pp of revenue. A = 10.0 (adoption already past 50%). These are consistent: high A reflects that AI adoption in this industry has already crossed 50% (GRIT 2025 confirms 67-72% current adoption), and the substantial V_raw of 13.47pp confirms meaningful automatable value exists. No tension to explain.

terminal_value.justification: AI cuts the researcher's costs AND partially replaces the researcher's product, but demand survives. Two mechanisms at work: (1) Cost-cutting: AI automates survey programming, QC, cross-tab analysis, report writing — shrinking project labor cost but not eliminating demand for primary fieldwork. (2) Product substitution: synthetic respondents and LLM analysis of existing data threaten primary fieldwork demand — but the mechanism holding demand is methodological trust. Buyers requiring verifiably human samples for legal/regulatory use (claims substantiation, litigation surveys), brand tracker continuity, and B2B expert opinion cannot substitute synthetically. The DIY precedent (SurveyMonkey/Qualtrics eroded the low end over 15 years but left primary research standing) suggests the industry is PRESSURED, not MELTING. MELTING requires that the product itself — paid access to real human respondents plus trusted interpretation — becomes replaceable, which requires regulatory acceptance of synthetic data for high-stakes uses. That has not occurred. Polling sub-segment (economically marginal) faces deeper pressure but is small relative to commercial research.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V (ai_replaceable_share) was derived from the prompt's hypothesis-based role structure and within-role automation fractions grounded in GRIT adoption data — no reference to M&A deal activity or consolidation trends. A (t50_years) was derived from GRIT 2025 current adoption data (72% of buyers, 67% of suppliers) and historical technology adoption analogs (online panels, DIY survey platforms) — no reference to deal activity.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Approximately 70% of industry revenue comes from fixed-price competitive project bids. When AI collapses programming/fieldwork/analysis costs, incumbent bid prices are competed down by AI-native entrants (Zappi-class, plus internal brand team insourcing). This is reflected in pi_dist = 0.40 (moderate-low client control, deflation channel active). The remaining ~25% tracker/retainer revenue is stickier but re-biddable when AI-native competitors offer equivalent trend data at lower cost. pi_moat = 0.40 reflects limited barriers: no licensure, DIY platform precedent established, switching costs only for tracker continuity. The overall C = 1.60 is a low capture score reflecting that most AI efficiency savings flow to clients via bid competition rather than accruing to the operator as expanded margin.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: V_raw ranges 0.0967-0.1773, V ranges 3.87-7.09. S ranges approx 3.96-4.77.

At buy=4.0, exit=9.0: M = clamp(4x5.0/4.0,0,10) = clamp(5.0,0,10) = 5.0; at buy=7.0, exit=9.0: M = clamp(4x2.0/7.0,0,10) = clamp(1.143,0,10) = 1.143. S ranges from approx 3.85 (low M) to 4.87 (high M).
C ranges from 1.20 (0.30x0.40x10) to 2.00 (0.50x0.40x10). S ranges approx 4.07-4.65.

evidence.reviewer_note: OEWS role_mix is entirely unusable: 4-digit 5419 is dominated by veterinary occupations from sibling NAICS 541940 (vet techs 9.2% wage share, vets 17.1% wage share). All role shares in ai_replaceable_share are hypothesis-based per prompt guidance, making quality LOW. The heterogeneous flag is false because 541910 is a relatively homogeneous code (commercial quantitative research dominates; public opinion polling is economically marginal). Confidence is LOW primarily due to role-mix contamination and ESTIMATE-quality multiples. The terminal value classification of PRESSURED rather than MELTING reflects that verified-human-sample demand (legal/regulatory/claims-substantiation) provides a floor, but the trajectory is clearly downward due to AI-native entrants, in-house insourcing via LLMs plus panel subscriptions, and the precedent of 15-year DIY platform erosion. A reviewer should verify whether synthetic data acceptance by regulators accelerates toward MELTING. Multiples are ESTIMATE: no specific 541910-sector deal database was accessible; advertising/marketing services agency comps (7-8x) and knowledge-services comps (Equiteq ESG avg 11.9x) were used to bracket the estimate. Active consolidators count of 3 is conservative; Savanta and STRAT7 last deals were 2023.
