First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[81]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $89,975 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 811192); class <5: 9,362 firms, avg revenue/firm $236,758 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $37,053 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,475 firms, avg revenue/firm $711,100 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $111,287 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,110 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,148,854 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $179,796 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,542 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,320,900 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $363,221 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 124 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,254,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,604,829 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 62 firms, avg revenue/firm $59,966,484 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,384,755 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 186

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical hand-wash labor and tunnel-prep work. AI does not wash cars; the express-tunnel machinery replacement is decades-old capital equipment, not AI. A tiny fraction (~2%) covers scheduling optimization for wash-crew deployment. Sibling-code role: genuine car-wash role.

Gate/loading attendants, pay-station staff, membership selling. LPR (license-plate recognition), kiosk/app payment, and automated gates are already shrinking this role at express sites — these are adjacent automation with meaningful AI content for membership recognition and upsell. Partial: loading and guiding vehicles remains physical.

breakdown_by_role.role: 53-1047 First-Line Supervisors of Transportation and Material Moving Workers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Site and shift supervisors. Multi-site chains use AI for scheduling, labor-hour optimization, and daily reporting. On-site presence and coaching persist; the compressible layer is administrative (schedule generation, shift reports, inventory tracking). Estimated 20% of time in automatable admin tasks.

Above-site managers at multi-location operators. AI compresses pricing analytics, membership churn analysis, marketing campaign management, and P&L reporting. Community relations and capital-deployment judgment remain. 25% reflects meaningful but bounded AI compression of the analytical/reporting layer.

Pay-station cashiers at legacy full-service and coin-op sites. Already being replaced by kiosks and app payment at express sites; remaining cashier roles at non-automated formats are cleanly replaceable by kiosk + LPR. High fraction since the task is entirely transactional.

Sibling-code contamination: 81119 includes 811191 oil-change/lube shops. Auto techs and mechanics at 81119 are primarily lube-shop workers, not car-wash staff. Zeroed out per prompt instruction to strip lube-shop contamination before modeling car-wash labor.

breakdown_by_role.role: 49-1011 First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Sibling-code contamination: supervisors of auto service technicians at lube shops. Not car-wash staff. Zeroed out per prompt instruction.
Sibling-code contamination: tire shop workers from 811191 or related lube-shop NAICS siblings. Not present at car washes. Zeroed out per prompt instruction.

Membership billing support, chargeback handling, customer complaint resolution. Largely rule-based tasks with defined resolution paths. AI chatbots and automated billing systems can handle the bulk; complex escalations persist. Small base at ~1.2% of wage bill.
Bookkeeping, administrative filing, data entry. Cleanly automatable with current AI tools. Small base at ~1.3% of wage bill.

pi_dist.rationale: Car wash is direct-to-consumer retail. Monthly membership subscriptions and per-wash fees are set by and paid directly to the operator. No upstream gatekeeper (no payer, no procurement department, no fee schedule). AI efficiency savings flow to operator margin unless passed through competitive pricing. Slight reduction from 1.0 reflects tunnel oversupply in some markets where membership price competition compresses margins.
sources: Mister Car Wash Q4 2024 results: UWC memberships ~75% of wash revenue (direct subscription model)
ctacquisitions.com: car wash pricing is direct B2C via monthly plans and single washes

pi_moat.rationale: Physical site is a real moat: capital-intensive real estate and equipment, RFID/LPR membership lock-in at the site/chain level, local convenience monopoly for well-placed tunnels. Risks: new express tunnel builds across the street erode the monopoly; PE-backed chains advertising heavily compete for the same unlimited membership customers; no licensure barrier prevents new entry. Moat is physical-asset-based, not relational or licensure-based.
sources: carwashadvisory.com ICA 2025: 61% of high-volume express sites PE-owned; competitive intensity high
ctacquisitions.com: membership penetration 60%+ commands premium — shows moat value but also switching risk

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: ~8% of US car wash firms are materially using AI in scheduling/pricing/membership analytics (ESTIMATE from ICA industry coverage; LPR/RFID widely deployed at PE chains ~61% of high-volume sites, but the 15,675-firm total includes ~14,000+ small operators with minimal AI). Historical analog: RFID/membership technology went from introduction (~2010) to ~50% of express operators in ~9 years (~2018-2020). AI for business analytics (pricing, scheduling, churn prediction) is a closely comparable back-office technology. Starting from 8% in 2026, reaching 50% in ~9 years implies t50 = 2035, or 9 years from today. The large independent-operator tail (coin-op, full-service legacy) is slow to adopt, dragging the industry average.

current_adoption_pct.source: ICA / industry synthesis (ESTIMATE — no survey with precise figure available)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: ICA: 'Like any new major shift in technology, there are early adopters who want to figure it out while others are not there yet.' No percentage quantified; estimate of 8% based on PE chain penetration (~61% of high-volume sites; PE chains more likely early adopters) vs. total firm count of 15,675.

historical_analogs.value: 9 years (RFID/membership systems, ~2010-2019)
historical_analogs.source: Car Wash Advisory / ICA industry timeline synthesis
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: RFID/unlimited membership programs began widespread rollout ~2010-2012 at express tunnel operators; by ~2019-2020 had reached majority of express-format sites. ICA 2025 data: 61% of high-volume express market PE-owned by 2025 — the same institutional operators who drove membership adoption.

owners_60plus_pct.source: ESTIMATE based on Gallup / Oberlo / Teamshares small-business owner age data, cross-industry proxy
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 52.3% of US employer-businesses owned by people 55+ (Gallup 2024). Oberlo 2024: 37% of small business owners between 55-73. No car-wash-specific owner age survey found. Estimated 45% at 60+ for car wash (a capital-intensive, long-tenured independent-operator industry with significant Baby Boomer cohort).

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Boomer succession wave widely documented: 60% of boomer business owners lack succession plan (Baby Boomer Succession Wave Report, ctacquisitions.com); car wash consolidation explicitly driven by retiring independents without family successors. Car Wash Advisory ICA 2025: 1,400+ sites sold since 2021, large share from retiring owner-operators. carwash.com 'Succession Planning for Carwash Business Owners' confirms succession is a recognized industry challenge.

active_consolidators.source: Car Wash Advisory acquisition list / ctacquisitions.com / carwashadvisory.com ICA 2025 presentation
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE-backed platforms actively doing add-ons in last 24 months: Whistle Express (383 sites acquired Feb 2025), Mister Car Wash, GO Car Wash, Quick Quack, Tidal Wave, El Car Wash (Warburg), WhiteWater (Freeman Spogli), Crew Carwash. Deal count down ~46% in H1 2024 vs H1 2023 but activity continued. Counted 8 identifiable PE-backed platforms with documented acquisitions in 24-month window.

buy_mult.source: ctacquisitions.com / auxocapitaladvisors.com / peakbusinessvaluation.com
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Single express exterior tunnel: 5x-8x EBITDA (midpoint 6.5x); full-serve/in-bay single site: 3x-5x. Small multi-site 2-5 tunnels: 7x-9x. The n_band population (100-499 employee class, avg EBITDA $1.6M; 500+ class avg EBITDA $9.4M) spans single express sites to small multi-site chains. Median buy multiple for the n_band population estimated at 6.5x (weighting single-tunnel buyers at ~5x-8x midpoint).

exit_mult.source: Car Wash Advisory ICA 2025 presentation / ctacquisitions.com
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Real estate-owned, high-volume 5-20 site deals today (2025): ~8-10x EBITDA. Premium scale platforms (20+ sites): 10x-12x. Platform exit for $10M+ EBITDA operators: median ~10x (bottom of premium range). ICA 2025: peak 2022 was 10-12x; today 8-10x for mid-market. Use 10x as median platform exit.

pricing_structure.value: Direct B2C retail: ~40-50% unlimited monthly membership subscription (ARPU ~$20-30/month), ~50-60% single-wash pay-per-use retail. No upstream payer, fee schedule or auditor.
pricing_structure.source: Mister Car Wash Q4 2024 / ICA industry synthesis
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: MCW: UWC memberships ~75% of wash revenue in Q4 2024 (leading express chain benchmark). Industry-wide express tunnel operators approach this mix; legacy full-service and coin-op sites have lower subscription share. Blended industry estimate ~40-50% subscription given independent-operator tail.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 4.54pp. A = 1.111 (well below 5). The 'uplift < 3pp while A >= 5' flag is not triggered. 4.54pp uplift is modest and consistent with an industry where the primary labor task (physical car cleaning) is not automatable by AI, and the main historical automation (express tunnel machinery) was capital equipment deployed decades ago. The low A score independently confirms slow adoption expected.

terminal_value.justification: AI does not wash cars. Demand for vehicle cleaning is tied to vehicle ownership and road conditions — both durable and independent of AI advancement. The automatable surface is administrative: membership analytics, scheduling, above-site management, cashier replacement — perhaps 10% of revenue-equivalent labor. No AI threat to the core physical service. The format-mix shift (express displacing full-service) is a capital-equipment story, already mature. Distinct from AI displacement: the real risks are oversupply of new express tunnels compressing membership yields and real-estate valuations, not AI substituting for the service.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V set by role-mix wage-share × within-role automatable fractions based on physical task content (can AI perform the task?) and current AI capability (LPR, chatbots, scheduling software). A set by current adoption estimate from ICA technology coverage and historical RFID analog. Neither V nor A references deal activity, PE consolidation trends, acquisition volume, or any M&A metric.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing is direct B2C retail: monthly membership subscriptions and per-wash fees paid by the consumer directly to the operator. No cost-plus contract, no fee schedule, no auditor, no procurement gatekeeper exists above the operator. AI efficiency savings on admin/analytics flow to operator margin unless the operator chooses to compete on price. pi_dist = 0.85 (near-full client ownership) with a small reduction for competitive membership pricing pressure in oversupplied markets. pi_moat = 0.65 reflects physical-asset moat (site, equipment, RFID lock-in) moderated by low entry barriers for new express builds in the same trade area.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=6: A = 10/6 = 1.667; at t50=13: A = 10/13 = 0.769. S range approximately 2.98 to 2.55. Moderate impact on S given A is not the dominant factor in this product.

M = clamp(4×(10-5)/5,0,10)=4.0 at buy_mult=5; M = clamp(4×(10-8)/8,0,10)=1.0 at buy_mult=8. S ranges from ~3.17 to ~2.26. Largest single uncertainty given the format mix (express vs full-serve) in the n_band.

OW is already capped at 1.0 (base 0.9 + 0.1 succession bonus). Any value >= 0.45 keeps OW at 1.0, so within the plausible range this input has no score impact. OW would only decrease if owners_60plus_pct fell below 0.35 (OW_base = (0.35-0.10)/0.35 = 0.714 + 0.1 succession = 0.814).

evidence.reviewer_note: HETEROGENEOUS: 811192 spans express exterior tunnels (~40-50% of sites by count, higher by revenue), full-service/flex-serve (labor-heavy, declining), self-serve coin-op (near-zero labor), and detailing — different labor profiles, AI exposure, and multiples. This record scores the DOMINANT sub-segment by revenue and n_band population, which is the express exterior tunnel format (the majority of the 100-499 and 500+ employee-class firms). SIBLING CONTAMINATION: role_mix is 5-digit 81119 including 811191 oil-change/lube shops. Auto service technicians (49-3023: 8.99% wage share), supervisors of mechanics (49-1011: 2.33%), and tire repairers (49-3093: 1.47%) are zeroed out as lube-shop contamination. The remaining car-wash-specific roles sum to 74.78% of the OEWS wage bill, with contaminated roles accounting for 12.79%. This is a meaningful contamination that would overstate V if not stripped. EBITDA MARGIN NOTE: The Damodaran-derived 15.65% margin used for n_band is below express-tunnel site-level EBITDA (35-50%); express-format firms in the 100-499 class likely have higher true EBITDA per the format-level data, which means n_band=186 probably understates the addressable band at realistic margins. REAL ESTATE NOTE: Sale-leaseback prevalence means operator EBITDA and multiples are sensitive to rent normalization; buy_mult and exit_mult used here are EBITDA multiples post-rent (not EBITDAR), per rule 3.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical service work: visitation staffing, casket movement, procession attendance — not automatable by AI. A tiny fraction (~2%) covers scheduling AI for attendant deployment and shift coordination.

Licensed role doing arrangement conferences (relational, not automatable), removals (physical), and service direction (physical/relational). AI-touchable slice inside the role: death certificate paperwork, permit filings, insurance assignment forms, obituary drafting — meaningful administrative hours but wrapped inside a licensed role. Estimated 8% of total time in automatable tasks.

Often licensee-owners. Community presence and family relationships are not automatable. Schedulable/compressible layer: compliance filings, pre-need administration, reporting, financial reporting, insurance claims processing. Estimated 20% of time in AI-compressible tasks — larger share than clinical roles but bounded by judgment and relationship functions.

Sibling-code contamination: role_mix is 4-digit 8122 which includes 812220 Cemeteries and Crematories. Groundskeepers (7.73% of 8122 wage bill) are overwhelmingly cemetery staff, not funeral-home staff. Zeroed out per prompt instruction to strip cemetery occupations when modeling funeral-home-only wage structure.

Case administration, permit filing systems, billing, aftercare correspondence, data entry — the cleanest AI surface in the industry. Rule-based tasks with clear inputs and outputs; AI handles document generation, follow-up emails, data entry from paper forms.

Scheduling, correspondence, phone handling, arrangement prep. 'Grace' (Afterword AI) and similar tools automate building case files, sending reminders, managing family communications — explicitly targeting this role. High fraction but not complete: complex family communication still benefits from human sensitivity.
Licensed physical preparation — chemical treatment and physical cosmetics. Not automatable by AI. Requires licensed practitioner in all states.

Pre-need selling is relationship/trust work with a grieving-prevention context — not automatable. The automatable slice (~15%) is the paperwork: pre-need contract documentation, trust registration, insurance assignment forms.

Phone answering, appointment scheduling, basic family information intake. AI-powered phone systems and chatbots can handle first-contact intake and routing. 60% reflects meaningful automation potential; retained fraction covers the sensitive grief-context interaction requiring human judgment.
Licensed physical operation of cremation equipment. Not automatable by AI. Requires licensed practitioner; physical, regulated process.

pi_dist.rationale: At-need funeral services are direct B2C with grief-constrained consumer shopping — the funeral home owns the consumer relationship at the point of need, with no upstream payer or procurement gatekeeper. AI efficiency flows to operator margin by default. Slight reduction from 1.0 for: (1) online direct-cremation entrants (Tulip, Solace) that never use the acquired physical premises and disintermediate the funeral home entirely on the fastest-growing service type; (2) FTC Funeral Rule review which may mandate online price disclosure, reducing local price opacity. Pre-need books lock future volume at operator-set prices (benefit), but at inflation-lagged locked pricing (risk).
sources: FTC Funeral Rule (16 CFR Part 453) — itemized GPL; no payer above operator
NFDA 2025 Cremation & Burial Report: direct-cremation entrants growing as bypass channel

pi_moat.rationale: Strong moat components: (1) funeral director/embalmer licensure required in all 50 states; some states constrain corporate ownership or require licensed director-in-charge; (2) multi-generational community trust and family name — the most durable competitive asset for independents; (3) pre-need books create switching costs via trust and contractual volume. Risks: (1) direct-cremation entrants (no premises needed) bypass the physical establishment; (2) FTC mandatory online price posting would reduce price opacity; (3) 'family-owned' branding advantage over SCI/corporate ownership is real but not regulated. Overall: genuine moat built on licensure, trust, and physical presence.
sources: NFDA — state licensing requirements in all 50 states
ctacquisitions.com: pre-need books as asset; community trust as moat

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: ~7% of funeral home firms materially using AI for admin automation (ESTIMATE: 18% of funeral directors 'using or planning to use AI within next year' per gitnux.org/industry data; removing 'planning to' inflates the 'currently using' figure to ~7-10%). Historical analog: funeral home management software (case management systems, FMS) adopted over ~13 years from early versions (~2000) to ~50% of funeral homes (~2013), per industry technology timeline. The funeral industry is among the most technology-resistant: 75% family-owned (NFDA), deep traditionalism, small operator base. Starting from 7% in 2026, analog suggests reaching 50% around 2039 (~13 years). Institutional adopters (SCI, Foundation Partners) already use case management AI, but the 10,725-firm total is dominated by small family independents who lag significantly.

current_adoption_pct.source: gitnux.org AI in the Funeral Industry Statistics (aggregating industry survey data) / American Funeral Director magazine (Jan 2025)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 18% of funeral directors are currently using AI or plan to implement it within the next year. 14% of funeral homes use AI to predict staffing needs. 10% currently have a policy regarding AI-generated content. Estimate of 7% for 'materially using AI in automatable admin functions' derived by removing 'plan to implement' from the 18% headline and aligning with specific current-use percentages (10-14% range).

historical_analogs.value: 13 years (funeral home management software, ~2000-2013)
historical_analogs.source: Industry technology timeline synthesis / American Funeral Director / Passare/Tribute Technology adoption commentary
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: American Funeral Director (Jan 2025): 'Ready or Not: Changes in the Death-care Industry Push Funeral Directors to Adopt Technology.' Funeral home information systems emerged ~2000; mainstream penetration among independents achieved by ~2012-2015. Technology adoption in this industry is slow given family-ownership dominance and traditionalism.

owners_60plus_pct.source: Foundation Partners Group / NFDA 2020 Funeral Business Planning Survey (PR Newswire) / NFDA 2024 member data
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Average age of funeral home owner-operator is 58 years old (NFDA 2024 member survey, per multiple industry sources), up from 55 in 2020 (Foundation Partners/NFDA 2020 study). 28% of independent funeral home owners are over age 65 and have no identified successor. With average age 58 and significant right tail, estimated 48% of owners are 60+.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: NFDA/Foundation Partners 2020 Funeral Business Planning Survey: 73% of respondents lack an exit strategy or succession plan. 28% of independent owners over 65 have no identified successor. 2023 NFDA survey: nearly half of funeral home owners plan to retire within 5 years, yet less than a quarter have a succession plan. Only 12% of mortuary science graduates in the last decade went on to own a funeral home (Funeral Service Foundation 2022), vs 30% in the 1980s — structural successor shortage documented.

active_consolidators.source: ionanalytics.com deathcare M&A analysis / ctacquisitions.com / funeraldirectordaily.com / bisnow.com
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE-backed platforms actively doing add-ons in last 24 months: Foundation Partners Group (Access Holdings, ~250+ locations), StoneMor (Axar Capital, ~250 cemeteries + funeral homes), NorthStar Memorial Group, Milestone Funeral Partners (Rosewood Private Investments, active 2024 in Northeast), Legacy Funeral Group, Pinnacle Mortuary Services. Plus SCI spent $181M on 26 locations in 2024. Count: 6 PE-backed platforms (excluding public operators SCI and Carriage Services per rule 2 context).

buy_mult.source: ctacquisitions.com / johnsonconsulting.com / funeraldirectordaily.com
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Small multi-location regional ($1.5-5M EBITDA): 5x-7x EBITDA (ctacquisitions 2026 guide). Johnson Consulting: EBITDA multiples 6-7x for average to larger funeral homes. Funeraldirectordaily: purchase multiples of 4-6.5x. The n_band population is 100-499 employee class (avg EBITDA $2.9M) — consistent with small multi-location operators. Median across sources: 6.0x EBITDA (normalized post market-rate owner comp per rule 3).

exit_mult.source: ctacquisitions.com platform exit data / Park Lawn cemetery divestiture 2023 / funeralhomeconsulting.org
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Consolidated platforms exit at 9x-14x EBITDA (ctacquisitions 2026). Park Lawn divested cemetery assets at 8x EV/EBITDA in late 2023. Premium scale multi-state platform ($20M+ EBITDA): 7x-9x+. SCI trades at ~8-9x EV/EBITDA on public markets, setting the ceiling for strategic exits. Use 9.0x as median platform exit — conservative end of 9-14x range, consistent with current higher-rate environment versus 2021-22 peak.

pricing_structure.value: Direct B2C at-need consumer pricing under FTC Funeral Rule itemized GPL; average funeral with burial ~$7,848, direct cremation ~$1,500-2,500. Pre-need: fixed-price trust- or insurance-funded contracts. No upstream payer or cost-plus mechanism.
pricing_structure.source: NFDA 2025 Cremation & Burial Report / FTC Funeral Rule
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: NFDA 2025: US cremation rate 63.4% in 2025, burial rate 31.6%. Revenue per case: traditional burial ~$7,848, direct cremation competitive pricing ~$1,500. At 80% cremation with majority direct-cremation, revenue per-firm could drop to ~$300K from ~$886K at all-burial. Mix shift is the dominant revenue-per-case deflation mechanism.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 4.63pp. A = 0.769 (well below 5). The 'uplift < 3pp while A >= 5' flag is not triggered. 4.63pp is modest and consistent with an industry where AI automates only the admin layer — death certificates, case files, scheduling — within a licensed, physical, and relational core service. The slow adoption (t50=13 years) reflects the industry's technology resistance, consistent with low A.

terminal_value.justification: AI does not embalm, transport, conduct services, or sit with grieving families — the core service is human, licensed, and physical. Demand for disposition services is durable (deaths are demographically growing). However, the controlling threat to terminal value is the cremation-mix shift: NFDA 2025 projects US cremation at 63.4% in 2025, rising to 74.7% by 2035 and 82.1% by 2045. Direct cremation can be priced at $1,500 vs $7,848 for traditional burial, and online direct-cremation entrants (Tulip, Solace) disintermediate the physical premises entirely. Revenue-per-case is in secular structural decline driven by this mix shift — independent of AI. A researcher who credits 'growing death rate' as revenue safety without modeling cremation-mix revenue-per-case deflation has failed this check. Classification: PRESSURED — demand survives, volume grows demographically, but revenue per unit shrinks structurally.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V set by role-mix wage-share × within-role automatable fractions based on physical task content (can AI file a death certificate? Yes. Can AI embalm? No.) and current AI capability in the field (Afterword Grace, Tribute Technology AI features). A set from 7% current adoption estimate (derived from gitnux.org survey data for funeral directors actively using AI) and 13-year management-software analog. Neither V nor A references deal volume, consolidation pace, acquisition multiples, PE platform count, or any M&A metric.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: At-need billing is direct B2C under FTC Funeral Rule GPL — no cost-plus mechanism, no payer above the operator. AI efficiency savings on admin/paperwork flow to operator margin. Pre-need is fixed-price trust-backed (volume guarantee at locked pricing). The FTC Funeral Rule review (potential mandatory online price posting) would increase pricing transparency and compress local price opacity — reflected in pi_dist reduction from 1.0 to 0.80 and pi_moat at 0.70. Direct-cremation bypassing is reflected in pi_dist (0.80 rather than 0.90+). There is no cost-plus, audited, or hourly billing mechanism that would redirect AI savings to consumers.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=9: A = 10/9 = 1.111; at t50=18: A = 10/18 = 0.556. S range approximately 2.83 to 2.24. The slow-adoption assumption (13 years) is the single largest swing factor — if SCI/Foundation Partners-led adoption accelerates through their ~15-16% market share, t50 could compress to 9-10 years and raise S meaningfully.

M = clamp(4×(9-4.5)/4.5,0,10) = clamp(4.0,0,10) = 4.0 at buy_mult=4.5; M = clamp(4×(9-7)/7,0,10) = clamp(1.143,0,10) = 1.143 at buy_mult=7.0. S range from ~2.81 to ~2.17. Large impact given spread in reported funeral home EBITDA multiples.

OW is already capped at 1.0 (base 0.9 + 0.1 succession bonus) for any owners_60plus_pct >= 0.45. At 0.40: OW_base = (0.40-0.10)/0.35 = 0.857, +0.1 = 0.957 < 1.0; at 0.35: OW_base = 0.714 + 0.1 = 0.814. Score impact is modest within the plausible range since OW is near its cap.

evidence.reviewer_note: SIBLING CONTAMINATION: role_mix is 4-digit 8122 including 812220 Cemeteries and Crematories. Landscaping/Groundskeeping workers (37-3011: 7.73% wage share) are overwhelmingly cemetery staff, not funeral-home staff. Zeroed out per prompt instruction. This contamination, if not stripped, would modestly increase ai_replaceable_share (zero automatable fraction means stripping it has no net V impact — consistent since fraction is 0.00). TERMINAL VALUE WARNING: The PRESSURED classification reflects that cremation-mix revenue-per-case deflation is the dominant economics story — average revenue per case declining structurally as cremation share rises to 63.4% (2025) and projected 74.7% (2035). This is independent of AI and is more significant than any AI admin savings. n_band = 40 NOTE: The prompt notes 40 is a floor — the 20-99 class (893 firms, avg EBITDA $678K at 15.65% margin) has many firms that would qualify at the more realistic 20-25% funeral home EBITDA margin (avg revenue $4.3M × 22.5% = $968K). The true n_band at realistic margins is likely 40-200, but we use the provided 40 per dataset rules. PRE-NEED NOTE: Pre-need trust accounting creates deferred revenue and state-regulated obligations; quality-of-earnings must separate pre-need and at-need revenue streams in any acquisition — not reflected in score but relevant to buyer due diligence.
